did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a deep learning algorithm (HUVY) for melanoma classification. The model was trained on the ISIC dataset (65,109 images) using an ensemble of state-of-the-art deep learning models and an optimization strategy to create a 'doubtful' category. It was tested on dermoscopic and tele-dermatology datasets to classify melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes the development, training, and validation of a novel deep learning algorithm (HUVY) for melanoma classification. It presents original experimental results, including model training on the ISIC dataset and performance evaluation on the GLOMEL, Dermatologists, and TeleExp datasets, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Performance of a Deep Learning Algorithm for Melanoma Classification Across Diverse Dermoscopic and Tele‐Dermatology Datasets
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated HUVY, a deep learning algorithm for classifying melanoma versus non-melanoma pigmented skin lesions. The model was trained on a filtered ISIC dataset of 65,109 images using an ensemble of state-of-the-art models and an optimization strategy to introduce a 'doubtful' category for uncertain cases. Performance was assessed on the GLOMEL dermoscopic database, a dermatologist-collected dataset, and a tele-dermatology dataset of real-life images. The algorithm demonstrated robust performance across these diverse sources, with AUCs ranging from 0.861 to 0.923 in binary classification. The introduction of the 'doubtful' category significantly improved clinical utility by allowing the system to flag lesions requiring expert review while confidently ruling out clear benign cases. These results suggest HUVY can help primary care providers make more informed referrals and reduce unnecessary specialist demands.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for classification. The model was trained to identify melanomas, which constituted 10% of the training data (up-sampled). In the ternary classification, these are labeled as 'likely melanomas'.

The negative class in binary classification, comprising various benign pigmented lesions. In the ternary classification, clear benign cases are distinguished from 'doubtful' ones. The study focuses on pigmented lesions specifically.

An innovative third category introduced by the optimization strategy to identify lesions where the model lacks confidence. These cases require human expert review. This category is derived from the uncertainty between melanoma and non-melanoma predictions.
Mentioned in the Introduction as an example of a benign lesion in the pathway to melanoma, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the results.
Mentioned in the Introduction as an example of a benign lesion in the pathway to melanoma, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the results.
Mentioned in the Introduction as a common benign lesion frequently misinterpreted as suspicious, but not explicitly listed as a distinct experimental class in the results.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma (Binary) and Melanoma vs. Non-melanoma vs. Doubtful (Ternary)
all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and ternary classification (melanoma, non-melanoma, and a 'doubtful' category for uncertain cases). The primary task is classifying pigmented skin lesions into discrete diagnostic categories. The model outputs probabilities between 0 and 1, which are then thresholded to assign discrete classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset
datasets_used.description: A large public dataset of skin lesion images used for training the deep learning algorithm. The dataset was filtered to remove redundant or nearly identical images to prevent overfitting.

per_class.Melanoma: Approximately 6511 (10% of total, up-sampled during training)

use.training: Used to train the HUVY deep learning algorithm. The model was trained on an ensemble of state-of-the-art deep learning models. The melanoma class was up-sampled by duplicating images to address class imbalance.

datasets_used.name: GLOMEL Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database used for testing. It aggregates data from Mednode, PH2, Derm7pt, and Dermofit. Labels were determined by histopathological analysis or a consortium of dermatologists.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the binary classification performance of the algorithm against pathological results.

datasets_used.name: Dermatologists Database
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of dermoscopic images retrospectively collected from outpatient dermatology clinics in Nantes, Rennes, and Angers between September 2023 and September 2024. Images were taken by board-certified dermatologists in daily practice. Labels were determined by histopathological analysis.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on dermoscopic images collected by dermatologists.

datasets_used.name: TeleExp Database
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of real-life tele-dermatology images taken by general practitioners and sent to dermatologists for labeling. Collected between September 2023 and September 2024. Labels were determined by dermatologists from Nantes, Angers, and Rennes. Only 68 of the original 137 images were suitable for AI analysis after quality assessment.

use.testing: Used to evaluate the algorithm's performance on real-life clinical images in a tele-dermatology setting. The 'doubtful' category was also assessed in this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and assess the performance of the binary classifier across different thresholds for melanoma classification.

statistical_methods_used.method: Threshold optimization via under-constraint optimization algorithm
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine optimal decision thresholds (T_L and T_U) for the 'doubtful' category by controlling sensitivity within an alpha constraint (α=0.05) and targeting a specific sensitivity level (S=95%) on the validation set.
To evaluate the binary classification performance of the algorithm on the GLOMEL, Dermatologists, and TeleExp datasets.
To measure the overall discriminative ability of the model for binary and ternary classification tasks.
To quantify the proportion of images classified as 'doubtful' cases that were excluded from the final performance metrics to assess the impact of the ternary classification approach.

statistical_methods_used.method: VPP (Virtual Power Plants) and VPN (Virtual Private Network) calculation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess model performance metrics, likely representing Positive Predictive Value and Negative Predictive Value in the context of the study's specific terminology.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for comparing model performance. The term 'alpha' (α=0.05) was used as a constraint parameter for threshold optimization to control sensitivity, not as a significance level for statistical hypothesis testing.

GLOMEL.description: Public dermoscopic database (Mednode, PH2, Derm7pt, Dermofit) with 2672 images. Labels determined by histopathological analysis or dermatologist consortium.
GLOMEL.notes: Sensitivity was higher than the expected 95% set on the validation set. The algorithm demonstrated consistent performance. Excluding the 'doubtful' class led to high performance.

Dermatologists.description: Private dataset of 157 dermoscopic images collected from outpatient clinics in Nantes, Rennes, and Angers (Sept 2023-Sept 2024). Labels determined by histopathological analysis.
Dermatologists.notes: Performance remained robust. The method allows specialists to focus on critical or uncertain cases.

TeleExp.description: Private dataset of real-life tele-dermatology images taken by general practitioners. Original size 137 images; 68 suitable for AI analysis after quality assessment (excluded due to absence of dermoscopic picture, blurred images, or atypical anatomic areas). Labels determined by dermatologists from Nantes, Angers, and Rennes.
TeleExp.notes: Performance remains robust on tele-dermatology images. 15.8% of cases deemed 'doubtful' would still have required a second opinion. About 11 patients with high-risk lesions could have received faster care if AI was used before tele-expertise. 25.5% of TeleExp images were too poor for AI analysis, and 24.8% did not meet quality standards.

architecture_used.main_model: HUVY (Ensemble of three state-of-the-art deep learning models)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (models were specifically trained on dermoscopic images of pigmented lesions from the ISIC dataset)
architecture_used.input_size: Not reported (images were cropped to focus on central areas)

architecture_details.components: Three state-of-the-art deep learning models with different architectures and image sizes
architecture_details.approach: Supervised deep learning operating akin to the human brain to scrutinize images at the pixel level

training_details.dataset: ISIC dataset (65,109 unique labeled images after filtering)
training_details.loss_function: Loss function comparing predictions to ground truth labels
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Up-sampling of the melanoma class (minority class, 10%) by duplicating existing images
training_details.data_preprocessing: Removal of redundant or nearly identical images; cropping to focus on central areas

classification_or_output_head.type: Binary classifier outputting probability between 0 (non-melanoma) and 1 (melanoma)
classification_or_output_head.thresholding: Decision thresholds applied to output probabilities; optimized using an under-constraint optimization algorithm with alpha constraint (α=0.05) and target sensitivity (S=95%) to create a 'doubtful' category

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble of three state-of-the-art deep learning models
preprocessing_techniques: Filtering of the ISIC training dataset to remove redundant or nearly identical images to prevent overfitting.
Cropping of images to focus on central areas (specifically mentioned for training data).

Preanalytical quality assessment of tele-dermatology images, excluding those with absence of dermoscopic picture, blurred images, or atypical anatomic areas (e.g., ear, toe, palms, soles, mucosa, hairy scalp).
Exclusion of images classified as 'doubtful' from specific performance metric calculations (binary classification results) to assess the impact of the ternary classification approach.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The melanoma class (minority class, 10% of the dataset) was up-sampled by duplicating existing images to address class imbalance during training.
Redundant or nearly identical images were removed from the ISIC training dataset to prevent overfitting and avoid accuracy overestimation.

conclusions: The HUVY deep learning algorithm demonstrated robust performance in classifying melanoma versus non-melanoma lesions across diverse datasets, including public dermoscopic databases (GLOMEL), dermatologist-collected images, and real-life tele-dermatology images.

The introduction of a 'doubtful' category for uncertain cases significantly improved clinical utility by allowing the system to flag lesions requiring expert review while confidently ruling out clear benign cases, thereby enhancing both sensitivity and specificity compared to traditional binary classification.
HUVY has the potential to help primary care providers make more informed referrals by distinguishing between likely melanomas, 'doubtful' lesions, and clearly benign lesions.
By filtering out benign cases, the algorithm frees specialists from approximately two-thirds of demands on benign lesions, allowing them to focus on critical or uncertain cases.
The algorithm's performance remained robust on tele-dermatology images captured by general practitioners, suggesting its suitability for screening by primary care professionals.

A key limitation is the reliance on strict adherence to photography protocols; in the tele-dermatology dataset, 25.5% of images were too poor for AI analysis and 24.8% did not meet quality standards, highlighting the need for user training.

The generalizability of the findings may be limited by the underrepresentation of individuals with darker skin tones (Fitzpatrick IV–VI) and the geographic concentration of data collection in western France.
The study focused specifically on pigmented skin lesions, which narrows the scope of application but addresses the urgent public health need for rapid diagnosis of suspicious pigmented lesions.
important_remarks: The study is retrospective, and the authors explicitly state that prospective validation is required to assess true clinical impact.

The algorithm was trained on the ISIC dataset (65,109 images) but tested on datasets with limited geographic diversity (western France) and underrepresentation of darker skin tones (Fitzpatrick IV–VI), limiting generalizability.

In the TeleExp dataset, 25.5% of images were too poor for AI analysis and 24.8% did not meet quality standards, highlighting a significant dependency on strict photography protocols and user training.

The 'doubtful' category was created using an optimization strategy with an alpha constraint (α=0.05) and target sensitivity (95%) on the training set, rather than through formal statistical hypothesis testing on the test sets.
The study focused exclusively on pigmented skin lesions, excluding mucosal lesions, large lesions, tattoos, and Fitzpatrick skin grades IV–VI, which narrows the scope of clinical application.

Two authors (B. Boule, L. Schroder) and one (E. Demoulins) have conflicts of interest related to HUVY medtech startup (unpaid leadership, employer, consulting fees), while others declared no conflicts.

The model achieved high sensitivity (>95%) across datasets, but specificity varied significantly (e.g., 0.50 on the Dermatologists dataset), indicating potential for false positives in real-world dermatologist-collected data.
Data availability is restricted; pictures cannot be shared due to privacy regulations, limiting independent verification of the results.
